texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hardware accelerators like quantum annealers or neuromorphic chips are capable of finding the ground state of a Hamiltonian. A promising route in utilizing these devices is via methods from automated reasoning: The problem at hand is first encoded into maxsat ; then maxsat is reduced to max2sat ; and finally, max2sat is translated into a Hamiltonian. It was observed that different encodings can dramatically affect the efficiency of the hardware accelerators. Yet, previous studies were only concerned with the size of the encodings rather than with syntactic or structural properties.
texts.text: Hardware accelerators like quantum annealers or neuromorphic chips are capable of finding the ground state of a Hamiltonian. A promising route in utilizing these devices is via methods from automated reasoning: The problem at hand is first encoded into maxsat ; then maxsat is reduced to max2sat ; and finally, max2sat is translated into a Hamiltonian. It was observed that different encodings can dramatically affect the efficiency of the hardware accelerators. Yet, previous studies were only concerned with the size of the encodings rather than with syntactic or structural properties.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We establish structure-aware reductions between maxsat , max2sat , and the quadratic unconstrained binary optimization problem ( qubo ) that underlies such hardware accelerators. All these problems turn out to be equivalent under linear-time, treewidth-preserving reductions. As a consequence, we obtain tight lower bounds under ETH and SETH for max2sat and qubo , as well as a new time-optimal fixed-parameter algorithm for qubo . While our results are tight up to a constant additive factor for the primal treewidth, we require a constant multiplicative factor for the incidence treewidth. To close the emerging gap, we supplement our results with novel time-optimal algorithms for fragments of maxsat based on model counting.

texts.orig: The goal is to find the model that minimizes the weights of the falsified clauses. (Here and in the rest of the article, the exponents indicate the weights of the clauses.) It is well-known in the theoretical computer science literature that maxsat remains NP -hard if every clause contains at most two literals, i.e., max2sat is NP -hard [20]. This is in stark contrast to the satisfiability problem, which is well known to be solvable in polynomial time on such formulas [25]. The hardness result of max2sat imposes an encoding from maxsat into max2sat , which, however, is so far deemed as of purely theoretical interest. While there are well-established maxsat solvers [31, 22, 40] and specialized solvers for fragments like Horn formulas [30], we are not aware of any tool that actively utilizes binary clauses.

texts.orig: The Ising model approach gained momentum since it naturally appeared in various promising technologies simultaneously. Perhaps the best-known ipu is the quantum annealer by D-Wave [23], which claims to utilize quantum effects to find the ground state. The usability of quantum-based ipu s is, for instance, actively explored in satellite scheduling [21, 2] and for efficient coalition formations among LEO satellites [46]. Another area in which Ising machines naturally appear are neuromorphic chips [3] such as Intel's Loihi [19] or ibm 's TrueNorth system [1]. This hardware implements physical artificial neurons to perform computations, often in mixed analog-digital implementations. It promises low-energy computations [43] in radiation-resistant hardware [36], making it a promising technology for future space applications [4, 39]. The rising interest in Ising machines also led companies like Fujitsu Limited [32] or Toshiba [24] to develop ipu s based on classical hardware.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The problem underlying the ipu (find the minimum of H = ∑ n i =1 w i x i + ∑ n i =1 ∑ n j = i +1 x i x j w ij ) is called quadratic unconstrained binary optimization ( qubo ). Utilizing an ipu , thus, requires to encode problems into qubo s [14, 15]. A common route in doing so is to use well-established encodings into maxsat and to then encode maxsat into qubo [8, 13, 35, 48, 38]. While it was experimentally observed that the choice of the encoding can significantly impact the performance of the Ising machine [26, 49], surprisingly, no study has been performed so far that takes properties of the encoding into account other than its pure size. In particular, to our knowledge, there is no study that takes syntactic and structural properties of the given maxsat instance into account - even though it is known that treewidth-based approaches are competitive for maxsat [7] and that ipu s may only be usable with Hamiltonians that have small treewidth [47].

texts.orig: Our main results are fine-grained reductions between maxsat variants and qubo . In fact, we prove that these problems are equivalent under linear-time reductions that preserve the primal or incidence treewidth. The primal treewidht tw( ϕ ) is the treewidth of the graph G ϕ that contains a vertex for every variable and that connects variables that appear together in a clause; the incidence treewidth itw( ϕ ) is the treewidth of the variable-clause interaction graph I ϕ that contains a vertex for every variable and every clause and that connects variables to clauses that contain them - formal definitions are given in Section 2.1. Figure 2 provides a more detailed overview of Theorem 1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The following section provides the necessary background in propositional logic and structural graph theory. The subsequent section proves the main theorem as a series of reduction rules. Each of these rules obtains as input a formula of some fragment of maxsat and modifies it slightly. The proof of Theorem 1 boils down to showing that applying these rules exhaustively results in a max2sat or qubo instance, and that these rules can be applied exhaustively in linear time without increasing the input's treewidth. We extend this result with new upper bounds for fragments of maxsat using tools from probabilistic reasoning and conclude in the last section.

Each rule obtains as input a wcnf ϕ and is either applicable , in which case a new ϕ ′ is output, or not, in which case nothing happens. When applying a rule i , we always assume that all rules j < i are not applicable. In any reduction rule, α will refer to a fresh auxiliary variable produced by the application of the rule . To illustrate this idea, the following two rules transform any wcnf into the standard form in which all soft clauses are non-trivial and unit.

Then, on the chain from t to t ′ there is a bag containing x t and x t ′ , to which we can attach two new bags containing these variables and one of the clauses of (1).

Transforming ( T, χ ) to this special form before applying the encoding from above ensures that (3) contains at most three literals.

texts.text: In this section, we present stronger upper bounds for fragments of maxsat by reducing these problems to the model counting problem #sat , i.e., to the task of counting the number of satisfying assignments of a (unweighted) cnf . We establish Theorem 3 in the form of three lemmas, one per item in the statement. The variants are defined below.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · unary-maxsat : Weights are given in unary and are, therefore, polynomially bounded by the input size.

texts.orig: Lemma 9. There is a linear-time reduction from maxsat with weights given in unary to maxsat with weights at most one such that the incidence treewidth is increased by at most one.
texts.text: Lemma 9. There is a linear-time reduction from maxsat with weights given in unary to maxsat with weights at most one such that the incidence treewidth is increased by at most one.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let ϕ be a wcnf whose weight function w is given in unary. For every variable x ∈ vars( ϕ ) with w ( x ) > 1 we use w ( x ) fresh weighted variables w i with weight 1. We construct w ( x ) clauses of the form c i = ( x ∨ ¬ w i ) for every i ∈ { 1 , . . . , w ( x ) } . This ensures that we can only get the weight of x (via weighted variables w i ) in case x is set to true. Observe that this increases the incidence treewidth by at most 1, as the fresh variables w i do not need to occur in a common decomposition bag. Indeed, one can manipulate any tree decomposition of I ϕ by finding a bag containing x , attaching new bags containing { x, c i } , to which another child with { c i , w i } is added.

texts.text: 6 Conclusion and Future Research

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recent rise of Ising machines created a demand for efficient encodings into max2sat and qubo . Reductions from maxsat to max2sat were previously considered to be of only theoretical interest [5] and, surprisingly, have not been studied so far with structural parameters like treewidth in mind. It is surprising in the sense that solving a qubo using an ipu requires an embedding from the problem into the hardware, which may only be possible if the Hamiltonian has small treewidth [47]. Hence, if any part of the chain from maxsat to max2sat to qubo increases the treewidth non-negligible, using an ipu may not be feasible at all.

Hence, if any part of the chain from maxsat to max2sat to qubo increases the treewidth non-negligible, using an ipu may not be feasible at all.

texts.orig: From a practical perspective, the next natural step in this line of research is an experimental evaluation of the proposed approach using different ipu architectures and to compare these hardware accelerators to state-of-the-art maxsat solvers. From the automated reasoning perspective, it would be interesting to study maxsat solvers that actively utilize binary clauses. There may be benchmarks on which such tools have an advantage in conjunction with the proposed reductions.
